quotes.text: I was born in that part of Farmington which is now Berlin, in Dec. 1752, and have lived in same place to this time.
quotes.attribution: Samuel Cowles, sworn pension affidavit, Berlin Connecticut, September 19, 1832 (U.S. Pension Office)

quotes.text: Samuel Cowles is well known to the Court as a worthy, honest man, a man of truth and a wealthy farmer of Berlin.
quotes.attribution: Probate Judge Thomas Lee of Berlin Connecticut, certifying Samuel's 1832 Revolutionary War pension application

military_service.unit: Capt. Sloper's cavalry company, Col. Bull's Connecticut regiment, Danbury
military_service.notes: Four wartime call-ups documented in his 1832 pension affidavit: (1) July 1776, marched to New York City, ~3 weeks; (2) September 1776, Peekskill and White Plains, ~3 weeks, present during the campaign that lost Fort Washington; (3) October 1777, Peekskill and Red Hook NY, ~2 months under Capt. Sloper or his Lt. Seth Deming; (4) October 1779, New Haven Alarm under Capt. Sloper, ~1 week. Pension rejected under Act of 1832 because total service fell short of the six-month minimum. Served alongside brother Jabez Cowles (H00435).

narrative_blocks.header: Cavalry service through four Connecticut call-ups
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel rode in Capt. Sloper's cavalry company under Col. Bull of Danbury through four wartime call-ups: New York City in summer 1776, Peekskill and White Plains that fall, Red Hook in 1777, and the New Haven Alarm in 1779. His regiment was at White Plains during the campaign that ended with the loss of Fort Washington and 2,800 Continentals captured.

narrative_blocks.header: Served the Revolution with his younger brother Jabez
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel served his four wartime call-ups alongside his younger brother Jabez Cowles, both riding under Col. Bull of Danbury. Sixty years later the brothers signed each other's pension paperwork — vouching for a war the federal government had nearly forgotten.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and died on the same Kensington parcel
narrative_blocks.body: 'I was born in that part of Farmington which is now Berlin, in Dec. 1752,' Samuel testified in his pension affidavit, 'and have lived in same place to this time.' He died there at eighty-one without ever moving.

narrative_blocks.header: Held the second-row pew, served as fence viewer
narrative_blocks.body: When the Kensington meetinghouse was reseated in 1815, Samuel was assigned the second row — a precise marker of village standing in a colonial congregation. He served the parish as grand juror in 1802 and as fence viewer over several years.

narrative_blocks.header: His pension claim fell short of six months
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel filed a Revolutionary War pension affidavit in 1832 detailing each call-up by date and commanding officer. The Pension Office rejected it because his total service fell short of the six months required under the Act of 1832 — though Probate Judge Thomas Lee certified him to the court as 'a worthy, honest man, a man of truth, and a wealthy farmer of Berlin.'

James was made a tythingman of the Kensington church in 1804 at age twenty-six, charged with keeping order during services and reporting Sabbath-breakers. He moved up to grand juror by 1809, a steady ascent through Connecticut's lay civic offices.

narrative_blocks.header: Second wife buried beside her first husband
narrative_blocks.body: James married Catherine Morris Dunham in 1842, five years after his first wife Charlotte's death; Catherine had been widowed by James A. Dunham a decade earlier. When she died in 1889 at eighty-nine, she was buried beside Dunham, not James, in South Kensington Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Both his sons died unmarried, farming Kensington
narrative_blocks.body: James's two sons Charles Bronson and Levi A. lived their whole lives as Kensington farmers and never married, dying in 1859 and 1886. The family's surviving line ran through his daughters Mary Lawrence Botsford and Laura Woodruff Dayton.

burial.plot_notes: South Burying Ground (South Kensington Cemetery), Kensington, Berlin, Connecticut. James, his first wife Charlotte (Bronson) Cowles, and their two sons Charles and Levi all buried here per Cowles Family Genealogy.
South Burying Ground (South Kensington Cemetery), Kensington, Berlin, Connecticut. Merritt and wife Rosetta (Peck) Cowles both buried here per Cowles Family Genealogy.

military_service.notes: Served as private in Connecticut militia during the War of 1812 under Capt. Deming. Specific Capt. Deming not yet identified — closest geographic/period match in tree is I00368 Chauncey Deming (Farmington merchant, husband of H00614 Diadema Cowles Deming), but his career record shows merchant/banker not military service. Could be a son or unrelated Deming. Research lead, not wired CC.

narrative_blocks.header: Served the War of 1812 under Capt. Deming
narrative_blocks.body: Merritt served as a private in the Connecticut militia during the War of 1812 under Capt. Deming, three decades after his father Samuel and uncle Jabez had ridden the Revolution under Capt. Sloper. The Cowles family carried two American wars across two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived his wife by twenty-nine years
narrative_blocks.body: Merritt married Rosetta Peck of Berlin in December 1813. She died in 1843 at fifty-three; Merritt lived on at Kensington until 1872, twenty-nine years a widower. Both lie in South Kensington Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: His son Samuel Brainard kept the country store
narrative_blocks.body: Merritt's son Samuel Brainard Cowles served as Kensington's postmaster, kept the country store, and rose to colonel of militia — all before consumption killed him at thirty-two in 1851. The store passed out of family hands; the militia rank lived on.

burial.plot_notes: South Burying Ground (South Kensington Cemetery), Kensington, Berlin, Connecticut, beside husband Merritt Cowles.

narrative_blocks.body: Polly died at twenty-three on June 15, 1808 in Kensington, never married. Her tombstone stood in Kensington — Samuel and Mary outlived their youngest by twenty-six and thirty-three years respectively.
